summary:
The Beersheba culture (also called the Beer Sheva culture) was a Chalcolithic culture of the northern Negev desert region in present-day southern Israel, dated to approximately 4500–3800 BCE. It is considered a regional facies of the broader [[event:1571|Ghassulian culture]] but is distinguished by distinctive settlement patterns and artifact types. The Beersheba culture communities occupied semi-arid valley margins where they could practice small-scale irrigated agriculture in the dry river beds (wadis) while keeping herds of sheep and goats on the surrounding steppe.

Beersheba culture sites are notable for their subterranean architecture — an elaborate system of underground rooms, corridors, and storage chambers dug into the loess substrate, providing protection from the extreme summer heat of the northern Negev. Above-ground structures were also present, but the underground complex is the most distinctive feature of these settlements. The sites have yielded evidence of intensive copper working, with copper ore-processing facilities, crucibles, and finished copper tools and ornaments indicating specialized craft production.

The Beersheba culture participated in the broader network of [[event:1570|Chalcolithic Levant]] exchange that distributed copper objects, ivory, and other prestige goods across the region. Its communities represent an early example of successful human adaptation to semi-arid environments through a combination of agricultural innovation, animal husbandry, and resource exchange.
